answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Start with total = 0. Loop through every number in the list and add it to total. Finally, return total.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Do not use sum(). This question is for practicing loops and accumulation.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Assume the first element is the largest. Then compare every number with the current largest and update it when a bigger number is found.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Do not use max(). Use comparison inside a loop.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Assume the first element is the smallest. Check all elements one by one and update smallest whenever a smaller value appears.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Do not use min(). This is the reverse idea of finding the largest element.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use a counter. For every number, check if num % 2 == 0. If yes, increase the counter.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: The modulo operator % helps us check divisibility.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Check each element from left to right. If arr[i] is equal to target, return i. If the loop ends without finding it, return -1.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Linear search means searching one element at a time.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Create an empty result list. Start from the last index and move backward, appending each element to result.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Do not use reverse() or slicing. Practice index movement.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use a counter. Compare every element with target. Increase the counter whenever the element matches the target.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: This is a basic frequency counting problem.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use two pointers: one at the beginning and one at the end. Compare both characters and move inward.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: If any pair does not match, the string is not a palindrome.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Loop through the string. Convert each character to lowercase and check whether it is present in 'aeiou'.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Use ch.lower() so that uppercase vowels are also counted.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Create an empty dictionary. For every character, increase its count if it already exists, otherwise set its count to 1.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: A dictionary is useful when we need to store frequency.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Create an empty result list. Add an element only if it is not already present in result. This keeps the original order.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Using a set may change the simple beginner approach. Here, use a list check.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Track two values: largest and second largest. Update them carefully while ignoring duplicate largest values.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: If all values are the same, there is no second largest unique value.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Compare every element with the next element. If arr[i] > arr[i + 1], return False. Otherwise, return True after the loop.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Non-decreasing means equal neighboring values are allowed.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Compare adjacent elements from left to right. Swap them when the left element is greater than the right element.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: After one Bubble Sort pass, the largest element usually reaches the end.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use nested loops. In each pass, compare adjacent values and swap if needed. Repeat until the list becomes sorted.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Do not use sort() or sorted(). This question is for learning the sorting process.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use two loops. Pick one number with the first loop and another number with the second loop. Return True if their sum equals target.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Use j = i + 1 so that the same element is not used twice.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Store the first element. Shift every other element one position to the left. Put the stored first element at the end.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: For an empty list, return the list as it is.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use two pointers, one for each sorted list. Compare current values and append the smaller value to the result.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: After one list ends, append all remaining elements from the other list.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: First count the frequency of every character using a dictionary. Then loop through the string again and return the first character with count 1.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: The second loop is important because it preserves the original order.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use a counter. Increase it for '(' and decrease it for ')'. If it ever becomes negative, return False. At the end, it must be 0.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: This is a beginner-friendly version of a stack problem.
